topic: A person discovers their mentor has retired without saying goodbye
raw_output:
[story 1]

The silence in the office was the loudest thing I had ever heard. Dust motes danced in the shafts of afternoon sun slicing through the blinds, illuminating the empty chair where Arthur used to sit. I kept tracing the outline of his desk with my eyes, a familiar, tight knot tightening in my chest with every glance. He was gone. Just… vanished. No note, no final conversation, just this gaping void where guidance used to reside. I picked up a file, the paper feeling flimsy and meaningless in my hands. I slammed it down on the cluttered mahogany, the sharp sound echoing unnervingly in the quiet space. Why did he just walk away like that? It felt like a deliberate erasure, a final, petty dismissal of everything I’d poured into this endeavor.

[story 2]

I stood by the window, watching the city bustle below, the energy of the street feeling jarringly loud after the profound stillness of the workspace. My jaw was clenched so tightly I could feel the ache in my molars. Arthur’s absence hung in the air, thick and suffocating. I walked over to his filing cabinet, running my fingers over the cold metal, a strange, brittle anger bubbling up inside me. It wasn't just disappointment; it was a deep, simmering frustration that he hadn't bothered to acknowledge the years of late nights, the sacrifices I’d made under his tutelage. I wanted to shout, to demand an explanation, but my throat felt constricted, the words catching on a dry, bitter taste.

[story 3]

The reception area was sterile, too bright, utterly devoid of the comforting, lived-in atmosphere Arthur had cultivated. I stood by the potted ficus, my hands shoved deep into my pockets, my shoulders hiked up toward my ears. Every time I glanced toward the door, a wave of sourness washed over me, making my stomach clench. He hadn't even paused for a handshake, hadn't offered a single word of farewell. It was as if he’d simply decided his involvement had expired, a formality dropped without a second thought. I took a slow, deliberate breath, trying to force the tightness in my chest to ease, but the tension only coiled tighter, a hard, unyielding stone lodged beneath my ribs.
